title: Der Hase und der Igel
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 2, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 187. Text from Wikisource („Der Hase und der Igel (1857)“, twice proofread against the original). Fresh translation made directly from the German; 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The one hundred and eighty-seventh tale (KHM 187), told largely in Low German (Buxtehude dialect). A hedgehog, mocked for his crooked legs by a proud hare, wins a wager of a race by posting his identical-looking wife at the far end of the furrow, so that whichever end the hare reaches he is met with “Ick bün al hier.” The Low German dialogue is a feature — its forms are glossed with their High German equivalents.

sentences.t: Disse Geschicht is lögenhaft to vertellen, Jungens, aver wahr is se doch, denn mien Grootvader, van den ick se hew, plegg jümmer, wenn he se mie vortüerde (mit Behaglichkeit vortrug), dabi to seggen „wahr mutt se doch sien, mien Söhn, anners kunn man se jo nich vertellen.“
sentences.l: This story is mendacious to tell, boys, but true it is nonetheless, for my grandfather, from whom I have it, was always wont, when he recounted it to me (delivered it with relish), to say thereby “true it must be, my son, otherwise one could not tell it at all.”
sentences.i: This tale is a made-up thing to tell, lads — yet true it is all the same; for my grandfather, from whom I have it, whenever he recited it to me, would always say as he went “true it must be, my son, else there'd be no telling it at all.”
sentences.n: The whole tale is told in Low German (Plattdeutsch, the Buxtehude dialect), and this is a feature, not an error — expect the Low German forms throughout. Quick key you will meet constantly: is=ist, se=sie, aver=aber, denn=denn, mien=mein, ick=ich, hew=habe, to=zu, mutt=muß, doch=doch. “lögenhaft to vertellen” = High German “lügenhaft zu erzählen,” a tall tale to tell. The grandfather's paradox — a lie that must be true — is the tale's playful frame.

sentences.t: De Geschicht hett sick aber so todragen.
sentences.l: The story, however, came to pass thus.
sentences.i: This, however, is how the tale fell out.
sentences.n: “hett sick ... todragen” = High German “hat sich zugetragen,” came to pass; “hett” = hat, “sick” = sich.

sentences.t: Et wöör an enen Sündagmorgen tor Harvesttied, jüst as de Bookweeten bloihde: de Sünn wöör hellig upgaen am Hewen, de Morgenwind güng warm över de Stoppeln, de Larken süngen inn’r Lucht (Luft), de Immen sumsten in den Bookweeten un de Lühde güngen in ehren Sündagsstaht nah’r Kerken, un alle Creatur wöör vergnögt, un de Swinegel ook.
sentences.l: It was on a Sunday morning at autumn time, just as the buckwheat was in bloom: the sun had risen bright in the heaven, the morning wind went warm over the stubble, the larks sang in the air, the bees hummed in the buckwheat and the people went in their Sunday best to church, and every creature was merry, and the hedgehog too.
sentences.i: It was on a Sunday morning in the autumn season, just as the buckwheat was blooming: the sun had risen bright in the sky, the morning wind passed warm over the stubble-fields, the larks were singing in the air, the bees were humming in the buckwheat, and the folk were going in their Sunday finery to church, and every creature was merry — the hedgehog among them.
sentences.n: A cascade of dialect nouns: Sünn=Sonne (sun), Hewen=Himmel (heaven), Larken=Lerchen (larks), Immen=Bienen (bees), Lühde=Leute (people), Swinegel=Igel (hedgehog, literally 'swine-hedgehog'). The parenthetical (Luft) is the source's own gloss of Lucht. “tor Harvesttied” = zur Herbstzeit; Harvest (cf. English 'harvest') = Herbst.

sentences.t: De Swinegel aver stünd vör siener Döhr, harr de Arm ünnerslagen, keek dabi in den Morgenwind hinut un quinkeleerde en lütjet Leedken vör sick hin, so good un so slecht as nu eben am leewen Sündagmorgen en Swinegel to singen pleggt.
sentences.l: The hedgehog, however, stood before his door, had his arms folded, looked meanwhile out into the morning wind and warbled a little song to himself, as well and as poorly as, now just on a dear Sunday morning, a hedgehog is wont to sing.
sentences.i: The hedgehog, for his part, stood before his door with his arms folded, gazing out into the morning wind, and trilled a little tune to himself — as well or as ill as a hedgehog is wont to sing of a fine Sunday morning.
sentences.n: “harr de Arm ünnerslagen” = hatte die Arme untergeschlagen, had his arms folded. Note “to singen pleggt” = zu singen pflegt, is wont to sing — pleggen/pflegen (to be accustomed to) recurs in this tale.

sentences.t: Indem he nu noch so half liese vör sick hin sung, füll em up eenmal in he künn ook wol, mittlerwiel sien Fro de Kinner wüsch un antröcke, en beeten in’t Feld spazeeren un tosehen wie sien Stähkröwen stünden.
sentences.l: While he was still singing to himself so, half softly, it occurred to him all at once that he might well, meanwhile his wife was washing and dressing the children, stroll a little into the field and look on how his turnips were standing.
sentences.i: As he was still humming half under his breath, it struck him all at once that he might, while his wife washed and dressed the children, take a little stroll into the field and see how his turnips were coming along.
sentences.n: “füll em up eenmal in” = fiel ihm auf einmal ein, it suddenly occurred to him (einfallen). “antröcke” = anzöge, subjunctive of anziehen (dress). Stähkröwen = Steckrüben, swedes/turnips — the hedgehog's crop, which drives the plot.

sentences.t: De Stähkröwen wöören aver de nöchsten bi sienem Huuse, un he pleggte mit siener Familie davon to eten, darüm sahg he se as de sienigen an.
sentences.l: The turnips, however, were the nearest by his house, and he was wont to eat of them with his family, therefore he regarded them as his own.
sentences.i: The turnips were the nearest to his house, and he and his family were used to eating from them, so he looked on them as his very own.
sentences.n: “sahg he se as de sienigen an” = sah er sie als die seinigen an, regarded them as his own (ansehen als; die Seinigen = his own).

sentences.t: Gesagt, gedahn.
sentences.l: Said, done.
sentences.i: No sooner said than done.
sentences.n: “Gesagt, gedahn” = the High German proverb 'no sooner said than done' — one of the few lines here in standard German rather than Low German.

sentences.t: De Swinegel makte de Huusdöör achter sick to un slög den Weg nah’n Felde in.
sentences.l: The hedgehog closed the house-door behind himself and took the road into the field.
sentences.i: The hedgehog pulled the house-door to behind him and set off along the road into the field.
sentences.n: “makte ... to” = machte zu, closed (zumachen). “slög den Weg ... in” = schlug den Weg ein, took/struck out on the road (einschlagen).

sentences.t: He wöör noch nich gans wiet von Huuse un wull jüst um den Slöbusch, (Schlehenbusch), de dar vörm Felde liggt, nah den Stähkröwenacker hinup dreien, as em de Haas bemött, de in ähnlichen Geschäften uutgahn wöör, nämlich um sienen Kohl to besehn.
sentences.l: He was not yet quite far from home and was just about to turn up around the sloe-bush (blackthorn-bush), which lies there before the field, toward the turnip-field, when the hare met him, who had gone out on similar errands, namely to look at his cabbage.
sentences.i: He had not got far from home, and was just about to turn up past the sloe-bush that stands before the field, toward the turnip-acre, when the hare met him — out on much the same errand, namely to inspect his cabbage.
sentences.n: (Schlehenbusch) is the source's own High German gloss of Slöbusch. “bemött” = begegnet (here preterite, met). “uutgahn wöör” = ausgegangen war, had gone out.

sentences.t: As de Swinegel den Haasen ansichtig wöör, so böhd he em en fründlichen go’n Morgen.
sentences.l: When the hedgehog caught sight of the hare, he bade him a friendly good morning.
sentences.i: When the hedgehog spied the hare, he wished him a friendly good-morning.
sentences.n: “ansichtig wöör” = ansichtig wurde, caught sight of. “böhd” = bot (bieten). The greeting “go'n Morgen” = guten Morgen; the apostrophe swallows letters, so it tokenizes as 'go' + 'n'.

sentences.t: De Haas aver, de up siene Wies en vörnehmer Herr was, un grausahm hochfahrtig dabi, antwoorde nicks up den Swinegel sienen Gruß, sondern segte tom Swinegel, wobi he en gewaltig höhnische Miene annöhm, „wie kummt et denn, dat du hier all bi so fröhem Morgen im Felde rumlöppst?“
sentences.l: The hare, however, who in his way was a distinguished gentleman, and cruelly haughty withal, answered nothing to the hedgehog's greeting, but said to the hedgehog, taking on a mightily scornful expression, “how comes it then, that you are running about here in the field already at so early a morning?”
sentences.i: The hare, though — a fine gentleman in his own estimation, and monstrously stuck-up with it — gave no answer to the hedgehog's greeting, but said to him, putting on a most contemptuous air, “how comes it that you're running about the field at so early an hour?”
sentences.n: “up siene Wies” = auf seine Weise, in his way. “segte” = sagte; “annöhm” = annahm (annehmen, put on). “rumlöppst” = herumläufst, run around.

sentences.t: „Ick gah spazeeren“ segt de Swinegel.
sentences.l: “I am going walking,” says the hedgehog.
sentences.n: “Ick gah spazeeren” = Ich gehe spazieren. Note the storyteller's shift to the present tense “segt” (sagt) for lively dialogue — common in Grimm.

sentences.t: „Spazeeren?“ lachte de Haas, „mi ducht du kunnst de Been ook wol to betern Dingen gebruuken.“
sentences.l: “Walking?” laughed the hare, “it seems to me you could well use your legs for better things too.”
sentences.i: “Walking?” laughed the hare, “seems to me your legs might be put to better use than that.”
sentences.n: “mi ducht” = mich dünkt / mir deucht, it seems to me. “Been” = Beine, legs (a sore point that sets off the whole quarrel).

sentences.t: Disse Antword verdrööt den Swinegel ungeheuer, denn alles kunn he verdregen, aver up siene Been laet he nicks komen, eben weil se von Natuhr scheef wöören.
sentences.l: This answer vexed the hedgehog enormously, for everything he could bear, but about his legs he would let nothing be said, precisely because they were crooked by nature.
sentences.i: This answer galled the hedgehog beyond measure, for he could put up with anything — but about his legs he would suffer not a word, for the very reason that they were crooked by nature.
sentences.n: “up siene Been laet he nicks komen” = auf seine Beine ließ er nichts kommen, an idiom: he would allow nothing said against his legs. “verdrööt” = verdroß (verdrießen); “scheef” = schief, crooked.

sentences.t: „Du bildst di wol in,“ seggt nu de Swinegel tom Haasen, „as wenn du mit diene Beene mehr utrichten kunnst?“
sentences.l: “You imagine, no doubt,” says the hedgehog now to the hare, “as if you could accomplish more with your legs?”
sentences.i: “You fancy, do you,” says the hedgehog to the hare, “that you can do more with your legs?”
sentences.n: “Du bildst di ... in” = du bildest dir ein, you imagine (sich einbilden). “utrichten” = ausrichten, accomplish.

sentences.t: „Dat denk ick“ seggt de Haas.
sentences.l: “That I think,” says the hare.
sentences.i: “That I do,” says the hare.

sentences.t: „Dat kummt up ’n Versöök an,“ meent de Swinegel, „ick pareer, wenn wi in de Wett loopt, ick loop di vörbi.“
sentences.l: “That comes down to a try,” means the hedgehog, “I wager, if we run in a race, I run past you.”
sentences.i: “That's to be put to the test,” says the hedgehog; “I'll bet that if we run a race, I'll outrun you.”
sentences.n: “up 'n Versöök an” = auf einen Versuch an (ankommen auf: to come down to). “pareer” = pariere (parieren), I bet. “in de Wett loopt” = in die Wette lauft, run a race/wager.

sentences.t: „Dat is tum Lachen, du mit diene scheefen Been,“ seggt de Haas, „aver mienetwegen mach’t sien, wenn du so övergroote Lust hest. Wat gilt de Wett?“ „En goldne Lujedor un’n Buddel Branwien“ seggt de Swinegel.
sentences.l: “That is laughable, you with your crooked legs,” says the hare, “but for all I care let it be, if you have such an overgreat desire. What is the wager worth?” “A golden louis d'or and a bottle of brandy,” says the hedgehog.
sentences.i: “That's a joke, you with your crooked legs,” says the hare, “but as far as I care, let it be, if you're so mightily keen. What's the stake?” “A gold louis d'or and a bottle of brandy,” says the hedgehog.
sentences.n: “mienetwegen mach't sien” = meinetwegen mag es sein, for all I care, let it be. Lujedor = Louisdor, a gold coin; Branwien = Branntwein, brandy.

sentences.t: „Angenahmen,“ spröök de Haas, „sla in, un denn kann’t gliek los gahn.“
sentences.l: “Agreed,” spoke the hare, “strike hands, and then it can go off at once.”
sentences.i: “Done,” said the hare, “shake on it, and then we can start right away.”
sentences.n: “Angenahmen” = angenommen, agreed. “sla in” = schlag ein, strike hands (on a bargain). “kann't gliek los gahn” = kann es gleich losgehen.

sentences.t: „Nä, so groote Ihl hett et nich,“ meen de Swinegel, „ick bün noch gans nüchdern; eerst will ick to Huus gahn un en beeten fröhstücken: inner halwen Stünd bün ick wedder hier upp’n Platz.“
sentences.l: “Nay, so great a hurry it hasn't,” meant the hedgehog, “I am still quite fasting; first I will go home and breakfast a little: in a half hour I am back here on the spot.”
sentences.i: “Nay, there's no such hurry,” said the hedgehog, “I'm still quite empty; first I'll go home and take a little breakfast — in half an hour I'll be back here on the spot.”
sentences.n: “Ihl” = Eile, haste. “nüchdern” = nüchtern, here on an empty stomach, not yet eaten. “upp'n Platz” = auf dem Platz, on the spot.

sentences.t: Damit güng de Swinegel, denn de Haas wöör et tofreeden.
sentences.l: With that the hedgehog went, for the hare was content with it.
sentences.i: With that the hedgehog went off, for the hare was satisfied.
sentences.n: “wöör et tofreeden” = war es zufrieden, was satisfied with it.

sentences.t: Ünnerweges dachte de Swinegel bi sick „de Haas verlett sick up siene langen Been, aver ick will em wol kriegen.
sentences.l: On the way the hedgehog thought to himself “the hare relies on his long legs, but I will get him all right.
sentences.i: On the way the hedgehog thought to himself, “the hare is trusting to his long legs — but I'll have him yet.
sentences.n: “verlett sick up” = verläßt sich auf, relies on (sich verlassen auf). “ick will em wol kriegen” = ich werde ihn wohl kriegen, I'll get him all right.

sentences.t: He is zwar ehn vörnehm Herr, aver doch man’n dummen Keerl, un betahlen sall he doch.“
sentences.l: He is indeed a distinguished gentleman, but yet only a stupid fellow, and pay he shall nonetheless.”
sentences.i: He may be a fine gentleman, but he's only a stupid fellow for all that — and pay he shall.”
sentences.n: “man'n dummen Keerl” = nur ein dummer Kerl, only a stupid fellow; 'man' = nur (only), a very common Low German particle.

sentences.t: As nu de Swinegel to Huuse ankööm, spröök he to sien Fro „Fro, treck di gau (schnell) an, du must mit mi nah’n Felde hinuut.“
sentences.l: When the hedgehog now arrived home, he spoke to his wife “wife, dress yourself quickly, you must out to the field with me.”
sentences.i: When the hedgehog reached home, he said to his wife, “wife, get dressed, quick — you must come out to the field with me.”
sentences.n: (schnell) is the source's own gloss of gau. “treck di ... an” = zieh dich an, get dressed (sich anziehen). “ankööm” = ankam.

sentences.t: „Wat givt et denn?“ seggt sien Fro.
sentences.l: “What is there, then?” says his wife.
sentences.i: “What's the matter, then?” says his wife.
sentences.n: “Wat givt et denn?” = Was gibt es denn?, what's going on?

sentences.t: „Ick hew mit’n Haasen wett’t üm’n golden Lujedor un’n Buddel Branwien, ick will mit em inn Wett loopen un da salst du mit dabi sien.“
sentences.l: “I have bet with the hare for a golden louis d'or and a bottle of brandy, I will run a race with him and there you shall be along with it.”
sentences.i: “I've made a wager with the hare for a gold louis d'or and a bottle of brandy; I'm to run a race with him, and you're to be there with me.”
sentences.n: “hew ... wett't” = habe gewettet, have bet. “mit'n Haasen” / “üm'n” / “un'n” — 'n is the swallowed article (den/einen); the apostrophe splits them into 'n' tokens.

sentences.t: „O mien Gott, Mann,“ füng nu den Swinegel sien Fro an to schreen, „büst du nich klook, hest du denn ganz den Verstand verlaaren?
sentences.l: “O my God, husband,” the hedgehog's wife now began to scream, “are you not sane, have you then quite lost your reason?
sentences.i: “O my God, husband,” the hedgehog's wife began to shriek, “are you out of your wits — have you clean lost your reason?
sentences.n: “füng ... an to schreen” = fing an zu schreien, began to scream. “büst du nich klook” = bist du nicht klug, are you not right in the head. “verlaaren” = verloren.

sentences.t: Wie kannst du mit den Haasen in de Wett loopen wollen?“
sentences.l: How can you want to run a race with the hare?”
sentences.i: How can you mean to race the hare?”
sentences.n: “loopen wollen” = laufen wollen; the modal 'wollen' at the very end is normal German verb-final order.

sentences.t: „Holt dat Muul, Wief,“ seggt de Swinegel, „dat is mien Saak.
sentences.l: “Hold your tongue, woman,” says the hedgehog, “that is my affair.
sentences.i: “Hold your tongue, woman,” says the hedgehog, “that's my business.
sentences.n: “Holt dat Muul” = halt das Maul, hold your tongue (Muul = Maul). Wief = Weib, woman/wife (not derogatory in this old register). “mien Saak” = meine Sache.

sentences.t: Resonehr nich in Männergeschäfte.
sentences.l: Don't argue in men's business.
sentences.i: Don't reason your way into men's affairs.
sentences.n: “Resonehr” = räsoniere (räsonieren), to argue/talk back. Männergeschäfte = Männergeschäfte, men's business.

sentences.t: Marsch, treck di an un denn kumm mit.“
sentences.l: March, dress yourself and then come along.”
sentences.i: Off with you — get dressed and come along.”
sentences.n: “Marsch” = a brisk command, 'off you go.' “treck di an ... kumm mit” = zieh dich an ... komm mit.

sentences.t: Wat sull den Swinegel sien Fro maken?
sentences.l: What was the hedgehog's wife to do?
sentences.i: What was the hedgehog's wife to do?
sentences.n: “Wat sull ... maken?” = Was sollte ... machen?

sentences.t: se mußt wol folgen, se mugg nu wollen oder nich.
sentences.l: she had to obey, whether she now would or not.
sentences.i: she had no choice but to obey, whether she wished it or not.
sentences.n: “se mußt wol folgen” = sie mußte wohl folgen, she had to obey. “se mugg nu wollen oder nich” = sie mochte nun wollen oder nicht.

sentences.t: As se nu mit eenander ünnerwegs wöören, spröök de Swinegel to sien Fro „nu pass up, wat ick seggen will.
sentences.l: When they were now on the way together, the hedgehog spoke to his wife “now pay attention to what I want to say.
sentences.i: When they were now on the way together, the hedgehog said to his wife, “now mind what I'm going to say.
sentences.n: “pass up” = paß auf, pay attention (aufpassen).

sentences.t: Sühst du, up den langen Acker dar wüll wi unsen Wettloop maken.
sentences.l: You see, on the long field there we want to make our race.
sentences.i: You see, on that long field we'll run our race.
sentences.n: “Sühst du” = siehst du, you see. “wüll wi” = wollen wir, we'll (here: let's). Wettloop = Wettlauf, race.

sentences.t: De Haas löppt nemlich in der eenen Föhr (Furche) un ick inner andern, un von baben (oben) fang wi an to loopen.
sentences.l: The hare namely runs in the one furrow and I in the other, and from up above we begin to run.
sentences.i: The hare, you see, runs in the one furrow and I in the other, and from the top we start running.
sentences.n: (Furche) and (oben) are the source's own glosses of Föhr and baben. “fang wi an” = fangen wir an, we begin (anfangen).

sentences.t: Nu hast du wieder nicks to dohn as du stellst di hier unnen in de Föhr, un wenn de Haas up de andere Siet ankummt, so röpst du em entgegen „ick bün all (schon) hier.“
sentences.l: Now you have nothing further to do than you place yourself here down below in the furrow, and when the hare arrives on the other side, then you call to him “I am already here.”
sentences.i: Now you've nothing more to do but station yourself down here in the furrow, and when the hare comes up on the other side, you call out to meet him, “I'm already here.”
sentences.n: (schon) is the source's gloss of all (= schon, already). The famous refrain “Ick bün all hier” = Ich bin schon hier, 'I'm already here' — the trick's punchline; you'll hear it again and again.

sentences.t: Damit wöören se bi den Acker anlangt, de Swinegel wiesde siener Fro ehren Platz an un gung nu den Acker hinup.
sentences.l: With that they had reached the field, the hedgehog assigned his wife her place and now went up the field.
sentences.i: With that they came to the field; the hedgehog showed his wife her place and then went up the field.
sentences.n: “wiesde ... an” = wies an, assigned/pointed out (anweisen). “anlangt” = angelangt, arrived.

sentences.t: As he baben ankööm, wöör de Haas all da.
sentences.l: When he arrived up above, the hare was already there.
sentences.n: “baben” = oben, up above; “all da” = schon da, already there.

sentences.t: „Kann et losgahn?“ seggt de Haas.
sentences.l: “Can it go off?” says the hare.
sentences.i: “Ready to start?” says the hare.
sentences.n: “Kann et losgahn?” = Kann es losgehen?, can we begin?

sentences.t: „Ja wol“ seggt de Swinegel.
sentences.l: “Yes indeed,” says the hedgehog.
sentences.n: “Ja wol” = jawohl, yes indeed.

sentences.t: „Denn man to!“
sentences.l: “Then to it!”
sentences.i: “Then off we go!”
sentences.n: “Denn man to!” = a Low German set phrase, roughly 'well then, get on with it!' — 'man' again = just/simply.

sentences.t: Un damit stellde jeder sick in siene Föhr.
sentences.l: And with that each placed himself in his furrow.
sentences.i: And with that each took his place in his furrow.
sentences.n: “stellde jeder sick” = stellte jeder sich, each placed himself.

sentences.t: De Haas tellde (zählte) „hahl een, hahl twee, hahl dree“ un los güng he wie en Stormwind den Acker hindahl (hinab).
sentences.l: The hare counted “half one, half two, half three” and off he went like a storm-wind down the field.
sentences.i: The hare counted “half one, half two, half three,” and off he went like a gale down the field.
sentences.n: (zählte) and (hinab) are the source's glosses of tellde and hindahl. “hahl een, hahl twee, hahl dree” is the counting cadence (halb eins, halb zwei, halb drei) used to start the race; twee = zwei, dree = drei.

sentences.t: De Swinegel aver lööp ungefähr man dree Schritt, dann duhkde he sick dahl (herab) in de Föhr un bleev ruhig sitten.
sentences.l: The hedgehog, however, ran only about three steps, then he ducked himself down in the furrow and stayed sitting quietly.
sentences.i: The hedgehog, though, ran only about three paces, then ducked down into the furrow and sat quite still.
sentences.n: (herab) glosses dahl. “duhkde he sick dahl” = duckte er sich herab, he ducked down. “bleev ruhig sitten” = blieb ruhig sitzen.

sentences.t: As nu de Haas in vullen Loopen ünnen am Acker ankööm, rööp em den Swinegel sien Fro entgegen „ick bün all hier.“
sentences.l: When the hare now, at full run, arrived down below at the field, the hedgehog's wife called out to meet him “I am already here.”
sentences.i: When the hare, at full gallop, arrived at the bottom of the field, the hedgehog's wife called out to him, “I'm already here.”
sentences.n: “in vullen Loopen” = in vollem Laufen, at full run. There it is again — “ick bün all hier” from the wife, indistinguishable from her husband.

sentences.t: De Haas stutzd un verwunderde sick nich wenig: he menede nich anders als et wöör de Swinegel sülvst, de em dat torööp, denn bekanntlich süht den Swinegel sien Fro jüst so uut wie ehr Mann.
sentences.l: The hare was taken aback and marveled not a little: he thought nothing other than that it was the hedgehog himself who called that to him, for as is well known the hedgehog's wife looks just so as her husband.
sentences.i: The hare was startled and no little amazed: he supposed it could be none other than the hedgehog himself who had called out to him — for, as everyone knows, a hedgehog's wife looks exactly like her husband.
sentences.n: “he menede nich anders als” = er meinte nicht anders als, he thought nothing other than. “süht ... uut” = sieht aus, looks; sülvst = selbst.

sentences.t: De Haas aver meende „datt geiht nich to mit rechten Dingen.“
sentences.l: The hare, however, thought “that doesn't go by rightful means.”
sentences.i: The hare, though, thought, “there's something not right in this.”
sentences.n: “datt geiht nich to mit rechten Dingen” = das geht nicht zu mit rechten Dingen, an idiom: 'it's not happening by fair means / there's something uncanny about it.'

sentences.t: He rööp „nochmal geloopen, wedder üm!“
sentences.l: He called “run once more, back again!”
sentences.i: He cried, “run it again — back the other way!”
sentences.n: “nochmal geloopen” = nochmal gelaufen, run again. “wedder üm” = wieder um, back around/again.

sentences.t: Un fort güng he wedder wie en Stormwind, datt em de Ohren am Koppe flögen.
sentences.l: And off he went again like a storm-wind, so that his ears flew about his head.
sentences.i: And away he flew again like a gale, so that his ears streamed back about his head.
sentences.n: “fort güng he” = fort ging er, off he went. “datt em de Ohren am Koppe flögen” = daß ihm die Ohren am Kopfe flogen; Koppe = Kopfe, head.

sentences.t: Den Swinegel sien Fro aver blev ruhig up ehren Platze.
sentences.l: The hedgehog's wife, however, stayed quietly on her place.
sentences.i: The hedgehog's wife, meanwhile, stayed quietly in her place.
sentences.n: “blev ruhig up ehren Platze” = blieb ruhig auf ihrem Platze.

sentences.t: As nu de Haas baben ankööm, rööp em de Swinegel entgegen „ick bün all hier.“
sentences.l: When the hare now arrived up above, the hedgehog called out to meet him “I am already here.”
sentences.n: The refrain now from the husband at the top — by alternating, the two hedgehogs are 'already there' at both ends every time.

sentences.t: De Haas aver, ganz uuter sick vör Ihwer (Ärger), schreede „nochmal geloopen, wedder üm!“
sentences.l: The hare, however, quite beside himself with vexation, screamed “run once more, back again!”
sentences.i: The hare, quite beside himself with rage, screamed, “run it again — back once more!”
sentences.n: (Ärger) glosses Ihwer (= Eifer/Ärger, here anger). “ganz uuter sick” = ganz außer sich, quite beside himself. “schreede” = schrie (schreien).

sentences.t: „Mi nich to schlimm,“ antwoorde de Swinegel, „mienetwegen so oft as du Lust hest.“
sentences.l: “Not too bad for me,” answered the hedgehog, “for all I care as often as you have a mind.”
sentences.i: “Nothing's too much for me,” answered the hedgehog, “as often as you please, for all I care.”
sentences.n: “Mi nich to schlimm” = mir nicht zu schlimm, roughly 'nothing too hard for me, I don't mind.' “so oft as du Lust hest” = so oft als du Lust hast.

sentences.t: So löp de Haas noch dreeunsöbentigmal, un de Swinegel höhl (hielt) et ümmer mit em uut.
sentences.l: So the hare ran seventy-three times more, and the hedgehog held out with him all the while.
sentences.i: So the hare ran seventy-three times more, and the hedgehog kept it up with him every time.
sentences.n: (hielt) glosses höhl. “dreeunsöbentigmal” = dreiundsiebzigmal, 73 times. “höhl et ... mit em uut” = hielt es mit ihm aus, kept it up / held out with him.

sentences.t: Jedesmal, wenn de Haas ünnen oder baben ankööm, seggten de Swinegel oder sien Fro „ick bün all hier.“
sentences.l: Each time, when the hare arrived down below or up above, the hedgehog or his wife said “I am already here.”
sentences.i: Every time the hare came up at the bottom or the top, the hedgehog or his wife said, “I'm already here.”
sentences.n: “seggten” = sagten. The trick is spelled out: one hedgehog at each end, so whichever way the hare runs, one of them is 'already here.'

sentences.t: Tum veerunsöbentigstenmal aver köm de Haas nich mehr to ende.
sentences.l: At the seventy-fourth time, however, the hare came no more to an end.
sentences.i: But at the seventy-fourth time the hare could go on no longer.
sentences.n: “veerunsöbentigstenmal” = vierundsiebzigstenmal, 74th time. “köm ... nich mehr to ende” = kam nicht mehr zu Ende, could not finish.

sentences.t: Midden am Acker stört he tor Eerde, datt Blohd flög em utn Halse un he bleev doot upn Platze.
sentences.l: In the middle of the field he crashed to the earth, so that the blood flew out of his throat and he stayed dead on the spot.
sentences.i: In the middle of the field he crashed to the ground, so that the blood burst from his throat, and he lay dead on the spot.
sentences.n: “stört ... tor Eerde” = stürzte zur Erde, fell to the ground. Blohd = Blut; “utn Halse” = aus dem Halse; “bleev doot upn Platze” = blieb tot auf dem Platze.

sentences.t: De Swinegel aver nöhm siene gewunnene Lujedor un den Buddel Branwien, rööp siene Fro uut der Föhr aff, un beide güngen vergnögt mit eenanner nah Huus; un wenn se nich storben sünd, lewt se noch.
sentences.l: The hedgehog, however, took his won louis d'or and the bottle of brandy, called his wife out of the furrow, and both went merrily together home; and if they have not died, they are living still.
sentences.i: The hedgehog, though, took his won louis d'or and the bottle of brandy, called his wife up out of the furrow, and the two of them went home together well pleased; and if they have not died, they are living yet.
sentences.n: “nöhm siene gewunnene Lujedor” = nahm seine gewonnene Louisdor. The closing “un wenn se nich storben sünd, lewt se noch” = und wenn sie nicht gestorben sind, leben sie noch — the standard fairy-tale ending ('...they live happily to this day').

sentences.t: So begev et sick, dat up der Buxtehuder Heid de Swinegel den Haasen dodt lopen hett, un sied jener Tied hatt et sick keen Haas wedder infallen laten mit’n Buxtehuder Swinegel in de Wett to lopen.
sentences.l: So it came about that on the Buxtehude heath the hedgehog ran the hare to death, and since that time no hare has again taken it into its head to run a race with a Buxtehude hedgehog.
sentences.i: And so it came to pass that on Buxtehude heath the hedgehog ran the hare to death, and from that time on no hare has ever again taken it into his head to run a race with a Buxtehude hedgehog.
sentences.n: “begev et sick” = begab es sich, it came to pass. “dodt lopen” = totlaufen, run to death. “hatt et sick ... infallen laten” = hat es sich einfallen lassen, has taken it into its head. Buxtehude is a real town near Hamburg — the tale localizes its tall tale precisely.

sentences.t: De Lehre aver uut disser Geschicht is erstens, datt keener, un wenn he sick ook noch so vörnehm dücht, sick sall bikommen laten, övern geringen Mann sick lustig to maken, un wöört ook man’n Swinegel.
sentences.l: The lesson, however, out of this story is firstly, that no one, even if he thinks himself ever so distinguished, should let it come to him to make fun of a lowly man, even were it but a hedgehog.
sentences.i: The moral of this story is, firstly, that no one — however grand he may think himself — should take it into his head to make sport of a humble man, even if it be but a hedgehog.
sentences.n: The closing double moral, still in dialect. “dücht” = dünkt (deems himself). “sick ... bikommen laten” = sich beikommen lassen, to take it into one's head (to presume). “wöört ook man'n Swinegel” = wäre es auch nur ein Igel.

sentences.t: Un tweetens, datt et gerahden is, wenn eener freet, datt he sick ’ne Fro uut sienem Stande nimmt, un de jüst so uutsüht as he sülwst.
sentences.l: And secondly, that it is advisable, when one marries, that he take a wife out of his own station, and who looks just so as he himself.
sentences.i: And secondly, that it is well advised, when a man marries, to take a wife of his own standing — one who looks exactly like himself.
sentences.n: “gerahden” = geraten, advisable. “wenn eener freet” = wenn einer freit, when one woos/marries (freien). “uutsüht” = aussieht; sülwst = selbst.

sentences.t: Wer also en Swinegel is, de mutt tosehn datt siene Fro ook en Swinegel is, un so wieder.
sentences.l: Whoever, then, is a hedgehog, he must see to it that his wife is also a hedgehog, and so forth.
sentences.i: So whoever is a hedgehog must see to it that his wife is a hedgehog too — and so on.
sentences.n: “de mutt tosehn” = der muß zusehen, he must see to it. The dry 'un so wieder' (und so weiter, and so forth) closes the tongue-in-cheek moral.
